smartlead_campaign: OR #3 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: something for LPC
thread.body_text:
hey Leilani, noticed LPC Golf's founder never touched leather or a sewing machine before starting, curious how that came about.

on a separate note: I built 3 meta ad mockups from LPC's own products, plus a world-class brand playbook from your site and reviews.

 no pitch attached, it's already built.

 should I send it?

- Kyle

 P.S. you gotta show people what an amazing brand you got for real!!

thread.subject: RE: something for LPC
thread.body_text:
Leilani - the part most brands react to isn't even the ads, it's the brand playbook. it lays out your voice, the exact language your customers use in reviews, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

it's all set to send alongside the mockups. want me to send both over?

- Kyle

I am open to a conversation.  

 Best, 
Leilani

Love it - as promised in my first email, here are your 3 ad mockups built from LPC's own products, plus your brand playbook. 

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

thread.subject: Re: RE: something for LPC
thread.body_text:
Hi Kyle,

I've booked some time on our calendar for Monday.

I had one quick piece of feedback on the mockups: the golf bag in image 2 appears way too small in comparison to the rest of the objects, but the others look great.

Also, just as a heads-up, our site is a custom Hydrogen build. I'm a software engineer, so I can implement changes myself, but please note that it is not a standard Shopify template.

Best,
Leilani

Leilani - fair call on the bag scale. the mockups were built from public site assets only, so think of them as angle concepts - with your actual product files they get a lot sharper. good problem to have.

 Hydrogen build is no issue at all, and having a software engineer on the brand side actually speeds everything up.

 talk Monday :)

 - Kyle

copy.tldr: LPC makes premium hand-stitched leather golf headcovers in San Francisco, they run zero ads right now, so this is a from-scratch launch built on craft and custom work, not a rescue job.

deep_dive.what_they_are: A small artisan brand making custom leather golf headcovers by hand in San Francisco. Legal name is Little Prince Customs. The maker is Karl. Leilani is your contact and she is a software engineer who can make site changes herself.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: Handcrafted, customizable leather golf headcovers. They have three collections named on the site: Quilted (20 designs), Exotic Classic (2 designs, embossed cowhide), and Artisan (8 designs, including rare-material pieces). They offer custom embroidery for initials, logos, or designs. No prices are public, so do not quote any.
deep_dive.positioning: The premium, heritage-craft end of the golf accessory market. Made by hand, made to last, made for you. Not mass-produced.
deep_dive.who_buys: Golfers who care about craftsmanship and personal style, and who will pay more for a handmade leather cover that stands out on the course and lasts.
customer_voice: These headcovers are absolutely amazing! The craftsmanship is top-notch and I get compliments on them every time I'm on the course.
strengths: Clear, real story: handmade in the USA by one artisan, Karl. That is hard to copy and easy to sell.
Custom embroidery and a build-a-set discount (10% off with 4+) give strong reasons to buy more than one.
gaps: Almost no visible social proof. One testimonial on the whole site. That makes cold traffic slower to trust.
No product feed or prices are public, and no ads are running now, so we are starting cold with no data to lean on.

game_plan.headline: Launch paid ads from zero, lead with the craft story and the Quilted collection, prove one winning ad before we spend up.

phases.goal: Prove that a paid ad can turn a cold golfer into a buyer at all. Find one ad and one product that works.
campaigns: One simple sales campaign to cold audiences interested in golf and golf gear.
A basic retargeting campaign for people who visit the site but do not buy, so we do not waste the traffic we pay for.
lead_products: Lead with the Quilted collection. It has 20 designs, far more than the other collections, which usually means it is the range they sell and reorder most. It photographs well and reads as classic and premium, which fits cold buyers who do not know the brand yet.
phases.creative: Start with the three ads from the Scroll-Stopper Sheet. Run the craft-story ad first, the one built on 'hand-stitched by one artisan in San Francisco.' It leads with what makes them different and does not need reviews to land, which matters since they have almost none. Do not run the mockup with the small golf bag (image 2) until we rebuild it with real product photos, as Leilani flagged the scale is off.
phases.budget: Start small and hold it steady while we learn.
phases.what_success_looks_like: One ad clearly beating the others, a handful of sales tied to it, and a clear read on which product people click. Not scale yet.

phases.goal: Put more money behind the ad and product that won, and widen who sees it.
campaigns: Scale the winning ad by lifting budget in steps, not all at once.
Test new lookalike and interest audiences built off the buyers we got in month one.
Add a review-collection push so we finally have social proof to put in ads.
lead_products: Keep the month-one winner as the lead. Introduce the build-a-set angle (10% off with 4+ covers) to lift order value, since a set is a natural buy for someone who liked one cover.
phases.creative: Refresh the winning ad with real product photos and any new customer quotes we collect. Test the custom-embroidery angle as a second hook, since personalization is a strong reason to buy.
phases.budget: Step budget up only on what is already working. Never scale a loser.
phases.what_success_looks_like: The winning ad still holds up at higher spend, order value ticks up from set sales, and we have at least a few new reviews to use.

phases.goal: Scale the proven setup and bring in a second product to widen the catalog we advertise.
campaigns: Scale the full winning setup across the widened audiences.
Introduce a second collection as a new lead ad. Test the Exotic Classic (embossed cowhide) as the premium standout, or the Artisan line for its rare-material pieces.
Keep retargeting running and layer in past buyers for repeat and set purchases.
lead_products: Add a second collection alongside the winner. Test Exotic Classic first because embossed cowhide is visually distinct and reads as a statement piece, which suits the premium buyer we are targeting.
phases.creative: Build fresh ads for the second product using the same craft-story frame that worked in month one. Do not reinvent the message, extend it.
phases.budget: Scale steadily on proven ads. Hold back spend on the new second product until it shows the same early signs the winner did.
phases.what_success_looks_like: Two products carrying sales instead of one, spend up from the start point, and a repeatable ad we trust.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.value: About $50 to $75 a day to start
estimates.basis: Enough to get a small but steady flow of clicks each day so we can tell which of the three ads works, without spending up before we have any proof. Kept modest on purpose because nothing is running yet and there is no past data.

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: We cannot give an honest number yet
estimates.basis: We have no prices, no past sales, and no conversion rate from them. A real target needs the average order value and what they can afford per sale. Get those on the call and we can put a real number to it.

assumptions: Their prices are high enough to carry paid ads. Handmade leather covers usually sell for a premium, but we have not seen a price. If the average order is low, the whole spend plan changes.
The Quilted collection is their strongest range. We are guessing this from it having 20 designs versus 2 and 8. If Karl says a different line sells best, we lead with that instead.
The site can take paid traffic and check out cleanly. It is a custom Hydrogen build, so we should confirm the buying flow works before sending paid clicks to it.
Custom items cannot be returned, which is fine, but it means the ad promise has to match the product closely so buyers are not disappointed.

questions.question: Have you ever run Meta or Instagram ads before, even a little, and if so what happened?
questions.listening_for: Whether they are truly starting from zero or stopped for a reason. A past failure tells you what not to repeat. 'Never tried' means expectations need setting slow.

questions.question: Roughly what does a headcover sell for, and what does a typical order come to?
questions.listening_for: The price and average order value. This is the single number the whole spend plan rests on. If they dodge it, gently explain you cannot set a real target without it.

questions.question: After materials and Karl's time, is there room to pay to get a new customer, or are margins tight?
questions.listening_for: Whether paid ads can even work here. Handmade means labor cost per item is high. If margin is thin, spend has to stay very lean.

questions.question: How many covers can Karl actually make in a week if orders pick up?
questions.listening_for: A supply ceiling. If Karl is a one-person shop, scaling ads too fast could bury him in orders he cannot fill. This caps how hard we push.

questions.question: Who runs marketing day to day right now, you or Karl?
questions.listening_for: Who the real decision maker is and who will be your contact. Leilani being technical is a plus, but confirm she owns this.

questions.question: When someone lands on your site today, do they usually buy, or do they leave? Any sense of that?
questions.listening_for: Whether the problem is getting traffic or converting it. If the site already converts organic visitors, ads have a real shot. If it does not, fix the page first.

questions.question: Which collection or design sells the most right now, and which do people ask about most?
questions.listening_for: Confirmation of the bestseller. This directly checks the assumption that Quilted leads. Change the plan if the answer is Artisan or Exotic Classic.

questions.question: Do you have real product photos and any customer photos or videos we can use in ads?
questions.listening_for: Whether we have assets to make sharp ads. The mockups were built from public images. Good photos and any customer clips make ads far stronger, especially with so few reviews.

questions.question: Do you collect reviews anywhere, and can we start asking recent buyers for them?
questions.listening_for: Whether social proof exists off-site or can be gathered fast. This is a known gap. If they have happy buyers we can reach, we fix it in month two.

questions.question: How long does a custom order take to make and ship?
questions.listening_for: The wait time. Long lead times need to be said clearly in ads so buyers are not surprised. It also affects how we message custom versus ready-to-ship items.

questions.question: Do you get people finding you on Google searching for things like custom golf headcovers or leather headcovers?
questions.listening_for: Whether search demand exists. People clearly do search for golf headcovers, so there is an honest opening for Google search ads once Meta is proving out. Do not oversell it before Meta works.

questions.question: How does your site show up when someone searches your brand or product by name?
questions.listening_for: Whether basic SEO and their product pages are set up to be found. A custom Hydrogen build can have SEO gaps. Worth a light look later, not a month-one priority.

objections.objection: We've never run ads and I'm not sure they work for something this handmade and pricey.
objections.answer: That is exactly why we start small and prove one ad first before spending up. You are not committing to big budgets. Premium handmade is actually a strength in ads, because the craft story gives people a reason to click that mass-produced brands do not have.

objections.objection: We barely have any reviews, won't that hurt the ads?
objections.answer: It is a real gap and I flagged it. That is why we lead with the craft-story ad first, which stands on its own without reviews. Then in month two we help you collect reviews from recent buyers so we can add real proof to the ads.

objections.objection: Karl makes these by hand. If ads work, we could get more orders than he can fill.
objections.answer: Good problem, and we plan for it. We start at a modest daily budget and only scale what is working, in steps. We match your ad spend to what Karl can actually make, so you never get buried.

objections.objection: Our site is a custom Hydrogen build, not a normal Shopify template. Will your plan even work with it?
objections.answer: No issue at all, and having you as a software engineer on the brand side actually speeds us up. The ad platform work is the same. We will just confirm the tracking and checkout flow are clean before we send paid clicks to it.

red_flags.flag: Almost no social proof on the site. Just one testimonial.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Cold buyers trust reviews. With almost none, ads have to work harder and may convert slower until we collect proof. Set expectations that month one is about learning, not scaling.

red_flags.flag: No prices or product feed are public.
red_flags.why_it_matters: We cannot confirm the average order value carries paid ads. If items are cheaper than we assume, the spend plan changes. Get real prices on the call before promising anything.
There may be a hard limit on how many orders can be filled. Scaling ads past that would create angry customers and refund pressure. Confirm capacity before pushing spend.

red_flags.flag: Custom items cannot be returned and customers pay return shipping.
red_flags.why_it_matters: This raises the bar on ads matching the product exactly. If an ad oversells, a buyer stuck with a non-returnable custom cover will be unhappy. Keep the ad promise tight and honest.

other_channel_openings: Google search ads are a real opening once Meta is proving out. People actively search for golf headcovers and custom leather headcovers, so there is existing demand to capture, not a category we would have to teach people about.
Light SEO check on their custom Hydrogen site later. Custom builds can miss basic search setup, and with a technical contact it is easy to fix. This is a month-three-plus item, not a priority now.
